name: Baingan Bharta
description: Smoky roasted aubergine mashed with tomatoes, onions, and spices â€” a rustic North Indian dish with an irreplaceable charred depth that can only come from open-flame roasting.
instructions: Roast aubergines directly over a gas flame, turning every 2-3 minutes, until the skin is completely charred and the flesh is soft â€” about 15-20 minutes. Alternatively, roast under a very hot grill.
Place in a bowl, cover with a plate, and let steam 10 minutes. This makes peeling easier.
Peel off and discard all charred skin. Mash the flesh coarsely with a fork, retaining some texture. Season with a pinch of salt.
Heat oil in a pan over medium-high heat. Add cumin seeds and sizzle 30 seconds.
Add onions and cook until golden-brown, about 12 minutes.
Add garlic, ginger, and green chilli. Cook 2 minutes.
Add tomatoes, turmeric, coriander powder, and salt. Cook until tomatoes are broken down and oil separates, about 8 minutes.
Add mashed aubergine. Stir well and cook on medium heat for 5 minutes to marry the flavours.
Finish with garam masala and fresh coriander. Serve hot with roti.
tips: The smoking over a direct flame is non-negotiable â€” it gives bharta its signature flavour. If you have an electric hob, use your oven grill at maximum heat and turn frequently. The more charred the skin, the smokier and better the result.

name: Palak Dal
description: Comforting yellow dal cooked with fresh spinach and finished with a sizzling cumin-garlic tadka â€” a nutritious, everyday lentil dish eaten across India with rice or roti.
instructions: Pressure cook toor dal with 2.5 cups water, turmeric, and a teaspoon of oil for 3 whistles (or simmer covered for 25 minutes until very soft).
In a separate pan, heat 2 tablespoons oil. Add onions and cook until golden.
Add garlic and ginger. Cook 2 minutes. Add tomatoes, chilli powder, and salt. Cook until soft.
Add chopped spinach and stir. Cook 4-5 minutes until wilted and tender.
Add the cooked dal to the spinach mixture. Stir and simmer together 5 minutes. Adjust consistency with water.
For tadka: heat 1 tablespoon oil in a small pan. Add mustard and cumin seeds. When they pop, add dried red chillies and sizzle 15 seconds.
Pour tadka over the dal. Serve immediately with rice or roti.
tips: Use fresh spinach, not frozen, for better texture and colour. Adding a squeeze of lemon at the end brightens the flavour considerably. Toor dal can be replaced with moong dal for a lighter, quicker-cooking version.

name: Moong Dal Halwa
description: A royal North Indian dessert â€” split yellow moong dal ground and slow-roasted in ghee until deeply golden, then cooked with milk, sugar, and cardamom into a silky, intensely flavoured halwa. A labour of love served at weddings and festivals.

ingredient_quantities.saffron: generous pinch, dissolved in 2 tbsp warm milk

instructions: Grind soaked, drained dal to a coarse paste with minimal water.
Heat ghee in a heavy-bottomed pan over medium-low heat.
Add the dal paste. Begin stirring immediately and continuously â€” the paste will splutter and spit.
Cook, stirring without stopping, for 30-40 minutes. The mixture will gradually dry out, granulate, and turn golden-amber.
When the dal is deeply golden and releases a nutty fragrance, it is ready. This step cannot be rushed.
Separately, warm milk and sugar together until sugar dissolves.
Carefully pour the warm milk mixture into the dal â€” it will splutter dramatically. Stir continuously.
Cook on medium heat, stirring, until all the milk is absorbed and the halwa reaches a soft, loose consistency.
Add cardamom powder and saffron milk. Stir through.
Garnish with nuts. Serve warm.
tips: The continuous stirring during the roasting phase is absolutely essential â€” any pause and the bottom burns. Use a thick-bottomed pan. The colour should be deep amber, almost like caramel â€” pale halwa tastes raw and floury.

name: Kerala Fish Curry
description: A vibrant, coconut-milk-based fish curry from Kerala, made intensely sour with kokum, perfumed with coconut oil and curry leaves â€” the essence of Malabar coastal cooking.

ingredient_quantities.firm white fish: 600g (king fish, snapper, or tilapia), cut into 2-inch pieces

instructions: Soak kokum in 1/4 cup warm water for 10 minutes. Extract the juice and discard the pieces.
Heat coconut oil in a clay pot or heavy pan. Add mustard seeds â€” let them pop.
Add curry leaves, sliced onions, green chillies, ginger, and garlic. SautÃ© until onions turn soft and golden, about 8 minutes.
Add tomatoes, turmeric, Kashmiri chilli powder, and coriander powder. Cook until tomatoes are soft and oil separates, about 5 minutes.
Pour in coconut milk and kokum juice. Stir well and bring to a gentle simmer.
Gently slide in the fish pieces. Do not stir â€” shake the pot gently to coat.
Simmer on medium-low heat, covered, for 12-15 minutes until fish is just cooked through. Do not overcook.
Taste and adjust salt and sourness. Finish with a drizzle of raw coconut oil and fresh curry leaves.
Rest 10 minutes before serving. Best with steamed rice.
tips: Cooking in a clay pot is traditional and adds a distinctive mineral note. Never stir the curry once fish is added â€” fish breaks apart easily. Kokum gives a unique fruity sourness that tamarind cannot fully replicate.

name: Rajma
description: Creamy red kidney beans slow-cooked in a rich onion-tomato-spice gravy â€” the iconic, hearty Punjabi dish that defines Sunday lunch in North Indian homes, always served with steamed basmati rice.

ingredient_quantities.kidney beans: 1.5 cups dried (soaked overnight) or 2 cans (400g each)

instructions: If using dried beans, pressure cook with 3 cups of water for 4-5 whistles until very tender. Reserve the cooking water.
Heat oil in a heavy pot. Add cumin seeds and bay leaf. Sizzle 30 seconds.
Add onions and cook until deep golden-brown â€” about 20 minutes. This is crucial for flavour.
Add garlic and ginger. Cook 3 minutes.
Add tomatoes, turmeric, coriander powder, chilli powder, and salt. Cook until oil separates, about 12 minutes.
Add cooked beans along with 1 cup of the cooking liquid.
Simmer on medium heat for 20-25 minutes. Use a ladle to mash some beans against the pot wall to thicken the gravy.
Add garam masala and crushed kasuri methi. Stir through.
Simmer 5 more minutes. Garnish with fresh coriander and a drizzle of ghee.
tips: Use dark red kidney beans (rajma), not light ones â€” they hold their shape better and have better flavour. Mashing some beans into the gravy is the traditional way to thicken it. Rajma always tastes better the next day.

name: Chicken 65
description: Fiery, vibrantly red deep-fried chicken from Chennai â€” marinated in yogurt and an aggressive blend of spices, fried until crackling crisp, and finished with curry leaves and green chillies. The quintessential South Indian starter.

ingredient_quantities.chicken: 500g boneless thigh pieces, cut into 2-inch cubes
ingredient_quantities.red food colour: 1/4 teaspoon (or Kashmiri chilli powder for natural colour)
ingredient_quantities.oil: for deep frying, plus 2 tablespoons for finishing

instructions: Combine all marinade ingredients â€” yogurt, ginger-garlic paste, food colour, all dry spices, cornflour, egg, salt, and lemon juice â€” with the chicken. Mix well and marinate at least 30 minutes (overnight in fridge is best).
Heat oil in a deep wok or pot to 175Â°C.
Fry chicken in batches for 5-6 minutes until cooked through and deep red-brown. Do not overcrowd.
Drain on a wire rack.
For finishing: heat 2 tablespoons oil in a wide pan over high heat. Add curry leaves â€” they crisp in seconds.
Add green chillies. SautÃ© 30 seconds.
Add fried chicken. Toss vigorously over high heat for 1-2 minutes.
Serve immediately with sliced onions, lemon wedges, and mint chutney.
tips: Double frying (fry once, rest 5 minutes, fry again for 2 minutes) gives a crunchier result. The finishing toss in curry-leaf oil is what gives Chicken 65 its signature South Indian character â€” do not skip it.

name: Medu Vada
description: South Indian lentil doughnuts â€” urad dal batter fried into crispy, airy rings with a cumin-and-pepper bite. Served with sambar and coconut chutney, they are the beloved breakfast of Tamil Nadu and Karnataka.
instructions: Drain soaked dal. Grind in a wet grinder or food processor with minimal water (2-3 tablespoons max) until very smooth and fluffy, about 5-7 minutes.
Transfer to a bowl. Add green chilli, ginger, pepper, cumin, curry leaves, onion, and salt. Mix well.
The batter should be thick enough to hold shape. Beat with your hand for 2 minutes to incorporate air.
Heat oil in a deep pan to 175Â°C.
Wet your hand. Place a portion of batter on your palm, poke a hole through the centre with your thumb, and gently slide into the oil.
Fry 3-4 at a time on medium heat, turning once, until evenly golden and crispy â€” about 5-6 minutes.
Drain on paper towels. Serve hot with sambar and coconut chutney.
tips: The batter must be very thick and stiff for the vadas to hold their ring shape. Beat the batter well â€” air incorporation makes them lighter inside. Wet hands prevent sticking when shaping.

name: Ven Pongal
description: A creamy, comforting South Indian breakfast of rice and moong dal cooked together in ghee with peppercorns, cumin, ginger, and cashews â€” simple, nourishing, and deeply satisfying.
instructions: Dry roast moong dal in a pan on medium heat until lightly golden and fragrant, about 3 minutes. Set aside.
Combine rice and roasted dal in a pressure cooker with 4.5 cups of water and salt. Pressure cook for 4 whistles until very soft and slightly mushy.
Alternatively, cook in a heavy pot covered for 30 minutes, stirring occasionally and adding water as needed.
Heat ghee in a small pan. Fry cashews until golden. Remove and set aside.
In the same ghee, add peppercorns and cumin. Sizzle 30 seconds.
Add ginger, curry leaves, and asafoetida. Cook 1 minute.
Pour this tempering into the cooked rice-dal mixture. Stir vigorously to combine.
Top with fried cashews and an extra spoon of ghee. Serve with sambar and coconut chutney.
tips: Pongal should be loose and porridge-like â€” if it thickens too much, add hot water and stir. The generous amount of ghee is essential; this is not the dish to cut fat. Use whole peppercorns for a more pronounced heat.

name: Besan Ladoo
description: Round, melt-in-the-mouth sweets made from chickpea flour slow-roasted in ghee with cardamom and sugar â€” one of the most beloved Indian mithai, made for festivals, celebrations, and everyday joy.
instructions: Heat ghee in a heavy-bottomed pan over medium-low heat.
Add chickpea flour. Stir continuously and cook for 18-20 minutes until it turns golden-amber, smells nutty and fragrant, and the raw flour smell is completely gone.
Remove from heat. Cool until just warm â€” not hot.
Add powdered sugar and cardamom. Mix well â€” the heat of the flour will melt the sugar slightly.
Add chopped cashews and raisins. Mix.
When the mixture is cool enough to handle but still warm (too cold and it won't bind), shape into smooth golf-ball-sized rounds by pressing firmly.
Cool completely on a tray. Store in an airtight tin up to 2 weeks.
tips: Roasting the flour properly is the most important step â€” under-roasted besan makes ladoos taste raw and bitter. The flour should turn a shade deeper than golden. Shape while still warm â€” once cold, the mixture crumbles and won't bind.

name: Sambar
description: South India's iconic sour-and-spicy lentil and vegetable soup, enriched with tamarind and sambar powder â€” the soul of Tamil Nadu, eaten at every meal with idli, dosa, rice, and medu vada.

ingredient_quantities.tamarind: lemon-sized ball, soaked in 1 cup warm water

instructions: Extract tamarind juice by squeezing and straining. Set aside.
Pressure cook toor dal until very soft. Mash smooth.
Heat oil in a large pot. Add mustard and cumin seeds. When they splutter, add dried red chillies, curry leaves, and asafoetida.
Add shallots and cook until soft. Add tomatoes and cook until mushy.
Add drum stick and aubergine. Cook 3 minutes.
Add tamarind juice, sambar powder, turmeric, and salt. Bring to a simmer.
Cook vegetables until tender, about 12-15 minutes.
Add mashed dal. Stir well, adjust consistency with water. Simmer 10 minutes.
Finish with fresh coriander. The sambar should be tangy, spicy, and deeply flavoured.
tips: The quality of sambar powder defines the flavour â€” homemade is far superior. Drumstick (moringa) gives an unmatched flavour; if unavailable, a combination of pumpkin and baby aubergine is excellent. Sambar improves significantly the next day.

name: Mango Lassi
description: India's most beloved cold drink â€” thick, creamy yogurt blended with ripe Alphonso mangoes, a touch of cardamom, and a pinch of saffron. The perfect accompaniment to a spicy meal.

ingredient_quantities.saffron: pinch, dissolved in 1 tablespoon warm milk

instructions: Peel and cube mango flesh. Blend until completely smooth.
Add yogurt, milk, sugar, cardamom, and saffron milk. Blend until frothy.
Add ice and blend briefly. Taste and adjust sweetness.
Pour into tall glasses. Garnish with a pinch of cardamom and a few saffron strands.
tips: Alphonso (Hapus) mango gives the best colour and flavour. Canned Alphonso pulp is an excellent year-round substitute. For a richer result, use hung curd (thick Greek-style yogurt). The saffron is optional but adds a beautiful golden hue.

name: Hummus
description: Silky, creamy chickpea and tahini dip from the Levant â€” when made from scratch with dried chickpeas and good tahini, it transforms into something rich, light, and ethereal, nothing like the commercial version.
instructions: Cook soaked dried chickpeas with a pinch of baking soda until very soft â€” about 1-1.5 hours. They should crush between fingers with no resistance. Reserve cooking liquid.
While hot, peel as many chickpea skins as you can â€” this makes hummus significantly smoother.
In a food processor, blend garlic and lemon juice for 30 seconds.
Add tahini and blend 2 minutes until pale and creamy.
Add the hot chickpeas and a pinch of salt. Blend for 3-4 minutes.
Add ice water, one tablespoon at a time, while blending. The cold water makes it light and airy.
Continue blending 4-5 minutes total â€” the longer you blend, the smoother.
Taste and adjust salt, lemon, and garlic.
Serve in a shallow bowl. Create a swirl with the back of a spoon. Drizzle with olive oil and sprinkle paprika and cumin.
tips: Hot chickpeas blended immediately make significantly creamier hummus than cold ones. Ice-cold water creates the smooth, airy texture. Good tahini is paramount â€” it should be nutty and pleasant to eat alone.

name: Falafel
description: Crispy, herbaceous Middle Eastern fritters made from ground raw chickpeas blended with parsley, coriander, cumin, and garlic â€” light and crunchy on the outside, soft and green within.
instructions: Drain soaked chickpeas. Do not cook them â€” raw, soaked chickpeas are essential for falafel.
In a food processor, blend chickpeas, parsley, coriander, onion, and garlic until a coarse, crumbly paste forms â€” not a smooth puree.
Add cumin, coriander, baking soda, salt, and flour. Pulse to combine.
Refrigerate the mixture for 30 minutes. This helps it firm up.
Heat oil to 175Â°C in a deep pan.
Shape into small patties or use a falafel scoop if available.
Fry in batches of 5-6, turning once, until deeply golden-brown â€” about 4 minutes.
Drain on paper towels. Serve immediately in pita bread with tahini sauce, pickled vegetables, and tomatoes.
tips: NEVER use canned chickpeas â€” the excess moisture makes falafel that falls apart in the oil. The mixture should be crumbly, not wet. If it seems too wet, add a tablespoon more flour. Falafel must be eaten immediately after frying.

name: Tom Yum Soup
description: Thailand's iconic hot and sour prawn soup â€” fragrant with lemongrass, galangal, and kaffir lime leaves, with a lip-tingling blend of chilli heat and lime sourness. The most requested Thai dish in the world.

ingredient_quantities.lemongrass: 3 stalks, bruised and cut into 2-inch pieces

instructions: Bring stock to a boil. Add lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime leaves, and chillies.
Simmer 5 minutes to infuse. The aromatics are not eaten â€” they flavour the broth.
Add mushrooms and tomatoes. Cook 3 minutes.
Add prawns. Cook 2-3 minutes until just pink. Do not overcook.
Remove from heat. Season with fish sauce, lime juice, and palm sugar.
Taste critically â€” it should be intensely sour, salty, and hot in balance. Adjust each component.
Ladle into bowls. Garnish with fresh coriander and extra lime. The aromatics are left in the bowl but pushed aside when eating.
tips: The balance of fish sauce (salty), lime (sour), and chilli (heat) is everything. Taste constantly and adjust. Tom Yum broth should be bold and assertive â€” never timid. For Tom Kha, add coconut milk for a creamier version.

name: French Onion Soup
description: The Parisian classic â€” onions slowly caramelised to a deep amber sweetness, simmered in beef stock and Cognac, then ladled into crocks and crowned with a gratin of toasted baguette and bubbling GruyÃ¨re.
instructions: Melt butter and oil in a large heavy pot over medium heat. Add all onions, salt lightly, and stir.
Cook onions, stirring every 5 minutes, for 45-60 minutes until deeply golden-amber and soft. Do not rush â€” this is the entire soul of the dish.
If onions stick, add a splash of water and scrape up the fond. The sugars at the bottom are flavour.
Add flour. Stir and cook 2 minutes.
Add white wine and Cognac. Stir vigorously, scraping the bottom.
Add stock, thyme, and bay leaf. Bring to a simmer.
Simmer gently for 20 minutes. Season with salt and pepper.
Ladle into oven-safe crocks. Place toasted baguette slices on top, then pile with grated GruyÃ¨re.
Broil under a very hot grill until cheese is bubbly, golden, and slightly charred at the edges â€” 3-4 minutes.
Serve immediately.
tips: The deeply caramelised onions are everything â€” 45 minutes minimum. A shortcut ruins the soup. Use the best beef stock you can find (homemade is ideal). GruyÃ¨re is traditional but ComtÃ© or Emmental work beautifully.

name: Shoyu Ramen
description: Tokyo-style soy-sauce ramen with a clear, amber broth built from chicken, soy, and dashi â€” rich but light, topped with chashu pork, soft-boiled egg, nori, and spring onions.

ingredient_quantities.spring onions: 5, whites for broth, greens for topping

instructions: Make broth: place chicken, kombu, ginger, garlic, and spring onion whites in a large pot. Cover with 3 litres of cold water. Bring to a boil, skimming foam. Simmer gently for 3 hours.
Make tare (seasoning): combine 4 tbsp soy sauce, 2 tbsp mirin, and 2 tbsp sake in a small pan. Simmer 5 minutes.

Make chashu: combine 6 tbsp soy sauce, 4 tbsp mirin, 2 tbsp sake, and 1 tbsp sugar. Roll pork belly, tie with twine, sear all sides in oil. Add to sauce and 1 cup water. Braise 90 minutes until tender. Cool in liquid, then slice.
Marinated eggs: boil eggs for 6 minutes 30 seconds for jammy yolk. Peel and marinate in 4 tbsp soy sauce, 2 tbsp mirin, diluted with water for at least 2 hours.
Strain chicken broth. Season with tare â€” 2-3 tablespoons per bowl.
Cook noodles per package. Drain and divide into bowls.
Ladle hot seasoned broth over noodles. Top with sliced chashu, halved marinated egg, nori, bamboo shoots, and spring onion greens.
Finish with a few drops of sesame oil and white pepper.
tips: Great ramen requires patience â€” the broth is where all the time goes. The tare is the seasoning, not the broth itself â€” this allows you to control salinity per bowl. A splash of chicken fat (skimmed from the broth) floated on top adds extraordinary richness.

name: Pozole Rojo
description: Mexico's ancient ceremonial soup â€” hominy corn simmered in a deep, dried-chilli broth with braised pork shoulder, finished with an extravagant garnish spread of shredded cabbage, radishes, lime, and tostadas.
instructions: Toast dried chillies in a dry pan 30 seconds per side until fragrant. Soak in hot water 20 minutes.
Blend softened chillies with 1 cup soaking water, half the onion, 3 garlic cloves, cumin, and oregano until very smooth. Strain for a silkier sauce.
Season pork with salt. Brown on all sides in oil in a large pot.
Add remaining onion, garlic, and stock. Bring to a boil, skimming foam. Simmer 60-70 minutes until pork is very tender.
Remove pork. Shred coarsely with two forks.
Strain broth. Return to pot. Add chilli sauce and hominy. Simmer 20 minutes.
Return shredded pork. Season with salt. Simmer 10 more minutes.
Serve in deep bowls. Pass garnishes: shredded cabbage, sliced radishes, dried oregano, lime wedges, and tostadas for crumbling in.
tips: Toasting and soaking dried chillies properly is the key to depth of flavour. The garnishes are not optional â€” they balance the richness of the broth. For Pozole Verde, replace the red chilli sauce with tomatillos and green chillies.

name: Spanakopita
description: Greek spinach and feta pie wrapped in many layers of shatteringly crisp, butter-brushed phyllo â€” fragrant with dill and spring onions, baked golden until every layer crackles.
instructions: Squeeze as much water from thawed spinach as possible â€” use a clean cloth and twist hard. Excess moisture makes soggy pie.
SautÃ© spring onions in olive oil until soft. Cool.
Combine spinach, feta, ricotta, eggs, spring onions, dill, nutmeg, and black pepper. Mix well. Taste â€” it should be well seasoned.
Preheat oven to 180Â°C. Brush a 30x20cm baking tray with melted butter.
Lay one sheet of phyllo in the tray, letting it overhang the edges. Brush generously with butter. Repeat with 7 more sheets, brushing each with butter.
Spread the spinach filling evenly over the phyllo.
Fold in the overhanging phyllo edges. Layer the remaining 8 sheets on top, brushing each with butter. Tuck in the edges.
Score the top into serving portions. Brush with remaining butter.
Bake 40-45 minutes until deeply golden and crispy. Rest 10 minutes before cutting.
tips: Keep unused phyllo sheets covered with a damp cloth â€” they dry out and crack in seconds. Squeezing every last drop of moisture from the spinach is non-negotiable. The pie improves greatly after resting â€” it firms up and the filling sets.

name: Risotto ai Funghi
description: Classic Italian mushroom risotto â€” Arborio rice coaxed to a creamy, flowing consistency through 18 minutes of continuous stirring, layered with dried porcini and fresh mushrooms, finished with Parmesan and butter.
instructions: Drain porcini, reserving the soaking liquid. Strain liquid through a fine sieve (to remove grit) and add to vegetable stock. Chop porcini roughly.
Heat olive oil in a wide, heavy pan. SautÃ© fresh mushrooms on high heat until golden-brown, 5 minutes. Season. Set aside.
In the same pan, reduce heat to medium. Add half the butter. Cook shallots until soft, 4 minutes. Add garlic and thyme.
Add rice. Toast, stirring, for 2 minutes until translucent at the edges.
Add white wine. Stir until completely absorbed.
Add warm stock one ladleful at a time, stirring gently but continuously. Wait until each addition is almost absorbed before adding the next.
After 12 minutes, add the porcini mushrooms and their strained soaking liquid.
Continue adding stock and stirring. At 18 minutes total, taste the rice â€” it should be al dente with a creamy, flowing consistency.
Remove from heat. Add remaining cold butter and Parmesan. Beat vigorously to emulsify â€” this is the mantecatura that makes risotto creamy.
Fold in sautÃ©ed mushrooms and parsley. Rest 1 minute. Serve immediately in warmed bowls.
tips: The porcini soaking liquid is liquid gold â€” strain it carefully and add it to the broth. Never rush risotto; the starches release gradually only with continuous, patient stirring. The final butter-and-Parmesan beat (mantecatura) creates the signature all-over creaminess.

name: Japchae
description: Korea's beloved glass noodle dish â€” sweet potato starch noodles stir-fried with colourful vegetables, mushrooms, and beef in a sesame-soy sauce, served warm or at room temperature for any occasion.
instructions: Boil glass noodles 6 minutes until tender. Drain, rinse with cold water, and cut into 15cm lengths. Toss with 1 tablespoon soy sauce and 1 tablespoon sesame oil.
Marinate beef: mix slices with 1 tablespoon soy sauce, 1 tablespoon sesame oil, minced garlic, and a pinch of sugar. Rest 15 minutes.
Blanch spinach 30 seconds, squeeze dry, season with 1/2 teaspoon sesame oil and salt.
Stir-fry each vegetable separately in a little oil until just tender. Season each with a pinch of salt.
Stir-fry marinated beef until cooked through, about 2 minutes.
Combine noodles, beef, all vegetables, and remaining soy sauce, sesame oil, and sugar in a large pan over medium heat. Toss together for 2-3 minutes.
Taste and adjust seasoning. The dish should be sweet-savory and lightly glossy.
Garnish with sesame seeds and egg strips if using. Serve warm or at room temperature.
tips: Stir-frying each vegetable separately preserves their distinct colour and texture. Glass noodles absorb sauce readily â€” do not over-season. Japchae improves after resting as flavours meld.

name: Prawn Biryani
description: Fragrant long-grain basmati layered with juicy prawns marinated in a spiced yogurt masala and cooked dum-style â€” a coastal celebration dish that brings together the best of the sea and the spice pantry.

ingredient_quantities.saffron: generous pinch in 3 tbsp warm milk

instructions: Birista: deeply fry 2 sliced onions until dark golden-brown and crispy. Drain on paper. These are essential.
Marinate prawns: combine with yogurt, ginger-garlic paste, turmeric, chilli powder, biryani masala, half the birista, salt, half the mint and coriander. Marinate 30 minutes.
Parboil rice: cook soaked rice in boiling salted water with whole spices until 70% cooked (still firm inside). Drain immediately.
In a heavy-bottomed pot, heat oil. Fry remaining onion until golden. Add tomatoes and cook until soft. Add the marinated prawns. Cook on high heat 4-5 minutes until prawns are almost cooked.
Spread remaining mint and coriander over the prawn layer.
Layer parboiled rice on top. Drizzle saffron milk and ghee over the rice.
Top with remaining birista.
Seal the pot tightly with foil, then the lid. Cook on high heat 3 minutes, then lowest heat 18-20 minutes (dum).
Rest 10 minutes before opening. Gently fold from the bottom before serving.
tips: Prawns cook very fast â€” they will finish cooking during the dum. If you overcook them before layering, they will become rubbery. The dum (slow steam cooking) is where the magic happens: seal the pot properly for maximum flavour.
